{"step_by_step_thinking": "In iron deficiency anemia, the body tries to increase iron uptake and utilization. This is achieved by increasing the expression of transferrin receptors on the cell surface, which are regulated by iron-responsive elements. When iron levels are low, the iron-responsive elements in the 5' untranslated region of the transferrin receptor-1 mRNA bind to iron regulatory proteins (IRPs), causing the mRNA to be destabilized and degraded. Conversely, when iron levels are sufficient, IRPs do not bind to the iron-responsive elements, allowing the mRNA to be translated into more transferrin receptors. Therefore, the iron-responsive elements in transferrin receptor-1 mRNA increase the concentration and synthesis of transferrin receptors. Apoferritin mRNA iron response elements, on the other hand, decrease ferritin synthesis when iron levels are high, as ferritin is a storage protein for excess iron.", "answer_choice": "A"}
